title: The Decline of Single-Earner Homebuyers in America
content:
To many Americans, the classic television family of *The Simpsons* seems increasingly unrealistic in today's economic climate. The most unbelievable aspect of the show is that the family affords a comfortable, two-story home solely on the wages of a single breadwinner with a high-school education. In reality, single-breadwinner homebuyers have become exceedingly rare across the United States. According to recent census data, more than three-quarters of young married couples buying homes in 1960 relied on a single income. Today, that proportion has plummeted to less than one in three, largely reflecting better job opportunities for women in the modern workforce.

Since the mid-twentieth century, women have broken down numerous barriers that previously kept lucrative jobs heavily male-dominated. Between 1960 and 2000, the labor-force participation rate of prime-age women surged dramatically from around forty percent to more than seventy-five percent. However, politicians and social commentators from across the political spectrum often lament the steady decline of the single-income family. Some worry that dual-income families inadvertently bid up the overall price of housing, healthcare, childcare, and local schooling. Consequently, many families now feel that two incomes are an absolute necessity just to maintain a basic middle-class lifestyle.

Interestingly, polling data suggests that about half of American mothers would actually prefer to stay at home rather than work. Statistical analysis reveals that in homeowning families, a wife's full-time labor participation rate sharply declines only when her husband's income reaches an exceptionally high threshold. This indicates that Americans must earn a very substantial amount before they feel comfortable relying on a single income. Ultimately, the modern necessity of dual incomes is not a straightforward story of hardship, but rather one of shifting economic trade-offs. If housing were significantly cheaper to build, America might finally see more single-income families achieving the traditional suburban dream.
